predictions.title: White House publishes frontier-model pre-release vetting executive order by Q3 2026
predictions.body: By Q3 2026 the May 4-5 New York Times reporting that the Trump administration is weighing an executive order creating a federal working group of tech executives + government officials to design a pre-release vetting process for new AI models — Anthropic, Google, and OpenAI briefed by senior administration officials last week, with Anthropic's Mythos cyber model (announced Apr 7, finding tens of thousands of zero-day vulnerabilities including a 27-year-old OpenBSD bug) cited as the immediate catalyst, Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei publicly framing a cyber "moment of danger," the UK NHS already ordering tech leaders to wall off hundreds of open-source GitHub repositories with a May deadline citing Mythos exposure, and the May 1 AISI cyber eval placing GPT-5.5 + Claude Mythos on the 32-step "The Last Ones" corporate-network attack range (each completing it end-to-end in 2-3 of 10 attempts against an AISI-estimated 20 human hours) — together force the White House to issue an executive order naming a structured pre-release vetting process for frontier models above a defined cyber-capability threshold ahead of the H2 2026 GPT-5.5 / Mythos / Opus 5 release cohort. The shape is most likely a working-group EO with disclosure + voluntary-test-protocol scaffolding (mirroring the May 2025 - Jan 2026 NIST AISI-equivalent posture) rather than a hard FDA-style pre-market approval regime; the structural force is that the Mythos Discord-leak incident and the NHS GitHub lockdown have made the absence of a federal pre-release review the politically operative gap. Anthropic Project Glasswing + AISI red.anthropic.com framework is the de-facto template the EO formalises; OpenAI + Google sign on within one filing cycle to avoid being the lone hold-out under the new working-group reporting cadence.

reasoning.because: NYT May 4-5 reports White House weighing pre-release-vetting EO; Anthropic + Google + OpenAI briefed by senior officials last week.
reasoning.given: Mythos zero-day discovery wave + AISI 32-step cyber-attack-range pass + UK NHS GitHub lockdown have made the absence of a federal pre-release review politically operative.
reasoning.so_that: White House publishes an EO naming a frontier-model pre-release vetting working group with disclosure + voluntary-test-protocol scaffolding above a defined cyber-capability threshold.
reasoning.landing: By Q3 2026; ahead of the H2 2026 GPT-5.5 / Mythos / Opus 5 release cohort.
reasoning.plain_language: After Anthropic's Mythos model found tens of thousands of software bugs and the UK health service shut down code repositories, the White House is set to publish a rule for vetting AI models before release.

predictions.summary: NYT reports the White House is weighing a frontier-model pre-release vetting EO after Anthropic Mythos triggered the UK NHS GitHub lockdown and the May 1 AISI 32-step cyber-attack-range pass. By Q3 2026 the EO is expected to formalise a working-group review with disclosure + voluntary-test-protocol scaffolding ahead of the H2 2026 release cohort.

predictions.title: AI-accelerator IPO cohort prices a second deal at >$10B by Q4 2026
predictions.body: By Q4 2026 the May 4 Cerebras Systems IPO launch — 28M shares at $115-$125 each to raise ~$3.5B at a $26.6B market cap at the high end (vs Series H $23B Feb 2026), $510M trailing revenue, OpenAI partnership disclosed at >$10B multi-year warrant + loan structure (Dec 2025 OpenAI $1B loan secured by warrants for >33M shares), angel roster including Sam Altman + Greg Brockman + Ilya Sutskever + Adam D'Angelo — combined with the standing $700B 2026 Big-Tech AI-capex tape, the OpenAI / Anthropic frontier-lab supply-contract precedent now publicly priceable on the open market, and the Fleet Data Centers $4.6B Storey County secured-notes offering as the parallel debt-issuance signal forces a second AI-accelerator IPO at >$10B valuation within the same calendar window — Groq + SambaNova + Tenstorrent + Rebellions + Etched the most-likely candidate cohort, with Groq the leading first-mover given its prior funding-round signaling and inference-tier API revenue. The forcing function is that Cerebras's IPO surfaces a public-market comparable for AI-accelerator-startup equity for the first time — buyside coverage now has a direct multiple to anchor against, so a sibling priced inside the same cycle reads as continuation rather than first-mover risk premium. The Cerebras pricing window itself acts as a forward-guide for sibling underwriters (Goldman / Morgan Stanley / Citi) who can syndicate the next deal at parallel terms. Bear case is Cerebras pricing below midpoint and forcing the cohort to wait two quarters; absent that, Q4 2026 lands the second deal.

reasoning.because: Cerebras May 4 IPO launch at $26.6B target valuation surfaces the first AI-accelerator-startup public-market comparable with disclosed >$10B OpenAI multi-year warrant deal.
reasoning.given: $700B 2026 AI-capex tape + Fleet Data Centers $4.6B secured-notes offering + frontier-lab supply contracts now public make the second AI-accelerator IPO a continuation play, not a first-mover risk premium.
reasoning.so_that: A second AI-accelerator IPO prices at >$10B valuation within the same calendar window; Groq is the most-likely first-mover candidate.
reasoning.landing: By Q4 2026; Goldman / Morgan Stanley / Citi syndicate the deal at parallel terms to Cerebras.
reasoning.plain_language: Cerebras just put a public price on AI chip startups, so within months a rival like Groq is set to IPO at over ten billion dollars.

predictions.summary: Cerebras' May 4 IPO launch at $26.6B with a disclosed >$10B OpenAI deal sets the first public-market comparable for AI-accelerator startups. By Q4 2026 a second AI-accelerator IPO is expected to price at >$10B valuation in the same window — Groq the most-likely first-mover candidate.

predictions.title: CISA publishes agentic-AI workflow-platform secure-deployment guide by Q3 2026
predictions.body: By Q3 2026 the cluster of LLM-workflow-platform CVEs landing in the same window — n8n CVE-2026-21858 (CVSS 10.0 unauthenticated RCE via webhook content-type confusion, codenamed Ni8mare by Cyera Research Labs, patched in 1.121.0, with Canadian Centre for Cyber Security + Australian ASD ACSC + Censys + Orca Security advisories), Flowise CSV Agent CVE-2026-41264 (prompt-injection-driven RCE where a chatflow user coaxes the LLM into emitting attacker-controlled Python), LMDeploy CVE-2026-33626 SSRF (first exploited within 12 hours 31 minutes of disclosure per Sysdig honeypot fleet), the Georgia Tech Vibe Security Radar count of 35 CVEs in March 2026 directly attributable to AI coding tools (with researchers estimating 5-10x undercount across the open-source ecosystem) — combined with the standing SGLang CVE-2026-5760 patch + the ~7,000 internet-exposed MCP servers (~half unauthenticated) baseline forces CISA to publish a dedicated agentic-AI workflow-platform secure-deployment guide naming n8n / Flowise / LangChain / LlamaIndex / Dify class platforms by category and prescribing webhook-content-type-validation + prompt-injection-aware least-privilege + MCP-tool-call audit-trail control families. The forcing function is that the Ni8mare CVSS-10 score on a no-code AI-workflow harness widely deployed in Tier-2 enterprises makes the existing CISA AI guidance (which targeted model providers and large-deployment-tier buyers) too narrow for the operational reality. The guide is most likely a CISA Cybersecurity Advisory or Secure-by-Design supplement; FBI + NSA + international Five Eyes counterparts co-sign within one cycle (the AL26-001 joint pattern from CCCS / ASD on n8n is the dispatch-shape precedent).

reasoning.because: n8n Ni8mare CVSS-10 + Flowise CSV-Agent prompt-injection RCE + LMDeploy 12-hour exploitation + 35 AI-coding-tool CVEs in March 2026 cluster on the no-code AI-workflow harness.
reasoning.given: Existing CISA AI guidance targets model providers + large-deployment-tier buyers; the Tier-2-enterprise no-code AI-workflow harness is operationally outside the published scope.
reasoning.so_that: CISA publishes a dedicated agentic-AI workflow-platform secure-deployment guide naming n8n / Flowise / LangChain / LlamaIndex / Dify class platforms with webhook-content-type-validation + prompt-injection-aware least-privilege + MCP-tool-call audit-trail control families.
reasoning.landing: By Q3 2026; FBI + NSA + Five Eyes co-sign within one cycle on the AL26-001 dispatch-shape precedent.
reasoning.plain_language: After several no-code AI workflow tools were hit by serious security flaws this month, the US cyber agency is set to publish a deployment guide naming those tools by category.

predictions.summary: Cluster of n8n CVE-2026-21858 (CVSS 10.0), Flowise CVE-2026-41264 prompt-injection RCE, LMDeploy CVE-2026-33626 12-hour exploitation, and 35 AI-coding-tool CVEs in March 2026 forces a published-standard response. By Q3 2026 CISA publishes an agentic-AI workflow-platform secure-deployment guide naming n8n / Flowise / LangChain / LlamaIndex / Dify class platforms.
